---
title: "Typhoid Prediction in Nepal"
subtitle: "Predictive Modeling of Typhoid Incidence in Nepal Under Extreme Climate Change Scenarios Using Machine Learning"
author:
  - name: Kritika Baral
    orcid: 0009-0007-1517-1063
    affiliations:
      - name: Kathmandu University
        department: Department of Health Informatics
        city: Dhulikhel
        country: Nepal
date: today
abstract: |
  Typhoid fever remains a significant public health challenge in Nepal, where
  monsoon-driven flooding and climate variability increasingly disrupt water,
  sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) systems. This study examined the
  spatiotemporal relationship between typhoid incidence and climate-induced
  extreme events across Nepal's 77 districts from 2015 to 2023 by integrating
  district-level outpatient surveillance data from the Health Management
  Information System (HMIS) with flood occurrence records and gridded climate
  variables, including precipitation, temperature, and relative humidity.
  Descriptive and correlation analyses, including lagged assessments, were
  conducted, followed by machine learning modeling using Random Forest (RF),
  XGBoost, and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) algorithms. An ensemble model
  combining all three algorithms was also evaluated. Typhoid incidence
  exhibited strong seasonality, with 70–80% of cases occurring during monsoon
  months (June–September) and showed a positive association with flood
  frequency (r = 0.334), which strengthened when floods preceded cases by one
  month (r = 0.412). Among the evaluated models, XGBoost achieved the best
  individual predictive performance (R² = 0.52), while the ensemble model
  achieved the highest overall accuracy (R² = 0.55). Flood-related features
  accounted for approximately 18% of explained variance. Projections under
  a medium-emissions scenario (SSP2-4.5) indicate a potential 25% increase
  in national typhoid burden by 2050, with disproportionate impacts in
  flood-prone Terai districts. These findings identify typhoid fever in
  Nepal as a climate-sensitive disease and highlight the importance of
  integrating climate indicators into disease surveillance and early
  warning systems to support climate-resilient public health planning.
keywords:
  - Typhoid fever
  - Climate change
  - Flooding
  - Machine learning
  - Extreme climate events
  - Nepal
  - Disease prediction
  - Public health surveillance
---

## Highlights

- **Problem.** Climate-driven year-on-year variability in typhoid incidence
  across Nepal's 77 districts is poorly characterised by existing
  surveillance systems, leaving health authorities reactive rather than
  anticipatory.
- **Approach.** Three machine-learning models (Random Forest, XGBoost,
  LSTM) plus an R²-weighted ensemble, trained on a 2015–2023 district-month
  panel integrating HMIS surveillance, ERA5-Land + CHIRPS climate, and
  DRR-portal flood records — with strict chronological 80/20 train/test
  split and TimeSeriesSplit CV.
- **Result.** Ensemble R² = **0.55** (XGBoost individual R² = 0.52);
  flood-related features account for **~18%** of explained variance;
  flood–typhoid correlation strengthens at a one-month lag (r = 0.412).
- **Projection.** SSP2-4.5 implies a **+25%** national typhoid burden by
  2050 (~469,000 annual cases); SSP5-8.5 implies **+40%**, with
  disproportionate impact on Terai districts.
- **Replication.** Every figure and metric on this site is loaded from
  the source code and data in the [Replication](replication/data.qmd)
  section.

## Read the paper

The full write-up follows the standard structure — sidebar links from
[Introduction](paper/01-introduction.qmd) onward. Headline results live in
[Results](paper/04-results.qmd) and the policy translation is in
[Discussion](paper/05-discussion.qmd).

## Reproduce the results

The [Replication](replication/data.qmd) section is end-to-end runnable:
data loading, feature engineering, model fitting, and evaluation. Clone the
repo, install dependencies (`make install`), then `make` regenerates the
entire site locally — including all charts and tables.

## How to cite

> Baral, K. *Predictive Modeling of Typhoid Incidence in Nepal Under
> Extreme Climate Change Scenarios Using Machine Learning*. Department
> of Health Informatics, Kathmandu University, Dhulikhel, Nepal. 2026.
> URL: https://baralsamrat.github.io
